perturbation.original_text: 1. Confidential Information is defined as any information, whether written or verbal, of either party hereto, (Disclosing Party) which is disclosed to o r observed by the other party (Receiving Party) in connection with or as a result of the evaluation of any possible transaction between the Univers ity of Arkansas at Little Rock and ______________________________ and which is, at the time of disclosure, marked as being Confident ial or Proprietary, or is reasonably identifiable as confidential, proprietary information of the Disclosing Party pertaining to information in the areas of (subject matter) : _______________________________________________ ___________________________. Such Confidential Information may include, but is not li mited to, business plans, forecasts, content, processes, projections or analysis, software, hardware, product, or system designs, specifications, documentation, code, structure, or protocols. Confidential Information that is disclosed verbally will also be included as proprietary.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. Confidential Information is defined as any information, whether written or verbal, of either party hereto, (Disclosing Party) which is disclosed to o r observed by the other party (Receiving Party) in connection with or as a result of the evaluation of any possible transaction between the Univers ity of Arkansas at Little Rock and ______________________________ and which is, at the time of disclosure, marked as being Confident ial or Proprietary, or is reasonably identifiable as confidential, proprietary information of the Disclosing Party pertaining to information in the areas of (subject matter) : _______________________________________________ ___________________________. Such Confidential Information may include, but is not li mited to, business plans, forecasts, content, processes, projections or analysis, software, hardware, product, or system designs, specifications, documentation, code, structure, or protocols. Confidential Information that is disclosed verbally will not be included as proprietary.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text now states that verbally disclosed confidential information will not be included as proprietary, directly contradicting the original definition which stated it would be. This creates uncertainty regarding the protection of verbally disclosed information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Arkansas Trade Secrets Act (Ark. Code Ann. § 4-75-601)
perturbation.law_explanation: The Arkansas Trade Secrets Act defines a trade secret broadly, including information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable. By excluding verbally disclosed information from the definition of 'Confidential Information,' the modified agreement may fail to protect information that qualifies as a trade secret under Arkansas law, potentially weakening the protection afforded by the Act.

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the original contract by stating verbally disclosed confidential information will *not* be included as proprietary, whereas the original text stated it *would* be. This misalignment in terminology regarding what constitutes confidential information, specifically whether verbal disclosures are included, creates a legal contradiction because it weakens the protection of such information.

perturbation.original_text: 8. This Non -disclosure Agreement is effective on the date specified in the Caption of the Agreement and will remain in effect for a period of five (5) years.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. This Non -disclosure Agreement is effective on the date specified in the Caption of the Agreement and will remain in effect until terminated by either party with thirty (30) days written notice.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the fixed term of 5 years to a terminable agreement creates ambiguity. Trade secret laws often consider the duration of protection when determining if reasonable efforts were made to maintain secrecy.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Arkansas Trade Secrets Act (Ark. Code Ann. § 4-75-601)
perturbation.law_explanation: The Arkansas Trade Secrets Act defines 'trade secret' partly based on whether efforts are made to maintain its secrecy. A short, easily terminated NDA may be seen as insufficient effort to maintain secrecy, potentially undermining trade secret protection under Arkansas law. The fixed term shows the intent of continuous security for a limited time.

perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies a fixed term of 5 years, while the changed contract allows termination by either party with 30 days' notice. The Arkansas Trade Secrets Act considers efforts to maintain secrecy. A short, easily terminated NDA may be seen as insufficient effort to maintain secrecy. Since the contract alteration undermines the efforts to maintain secrecy, it creates a legal contradiction. The snippets provided do not explicitly discuss trade secret law but the LLM generated explanation focuses on it.

perturbation.original_text: 7. This Agreement shall be governed, construed, and interpreted by the laws of the State of Arkansas without reference to its choice of laws principles.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. This Agreement shall be governed, construed, and interpreted by the laws of the State of Delaware, regardless of conflict of law principles.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from Arkansas to Delaware may impact the enforceability of the NDA, especially if the parties are primarily located in Arkansas and the confidential information is related to Arkansas activities. Choice of law provisions are generally respected if there is a reasonable relationship to the chosen jurisdiction, but this change removes that factor. Also, changing the state from which the original contract was drafted affects what the standard is for statutory definitions.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187 outlines the principles for determining the enforceability of choice-of-law provisions. While parties can generally choose the governing law, the chosen state must have a substantial relationship to the parties or the transaction, or there must be a reasonable basis for the parties' choice. If the parties' activities are primarily in Arkansas and there is no connection to Delaware, a Delaware choice-of-law provision may be challenged, especially regarding trade secret protection. In this case, that law will be superseded.

perturbation.justification: The change in governing law from Arkansas to Delaware, as explained in the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation, may create a legal contradiction. While parties can generally choose the governing law, the chosen state should have a substantial relationship to the parties or the transaction. If the parties' activities are primarily in Arkansas and there is no connection to Delaware, this choice of law may be challenged. The modified text directly changes the governing law, potentially leading to unenforceability if Delaware law is chosen without a reasonable basis. This is because the original law, Arkansas, has a reasonable relationship to the parties, whereas changing it to Delaware removes that. Although there are no scraped snippets to further support this, based on the explanation and definitions of the perturbation, it is clear that a legal contradiction was created.

perturbation.original_text: 6. No license, express or implied, in the Confidential Information is granted to either party other than to use the information in the manner and to the extent authorized by this Agreement. Each Party shall retain the t itle and full ownership rights to their respective “Confidential Information”.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. A limited, perpetual license to use the Confidential Information for internal research purposes is granted to the Receiving Party. Each Party shall retain the t itle and full ownership rights to their respective “Confidential Information”.
perturbation.explanation: Granting a 'perpetual license' to the Receiving Party undermines the purpose of an NDA, which is to protect the confidentiality of information. A perpetual license essentially allows the Receiving Party to use the information indefinitely, which contradicts the intent of restricting its use and maintaining its secrecy.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Arkansas Trade Secrets Act (Ark. Code Ann. § 4-75-601)
perturbation.law_explanation: Under the Arkansas Trade Secrets Act, a trade secret must be subject to efforts to maintain its secrecy. By granting a perpetual license, the Disclosing Party is essentially allowing the Receiving Party to use the information indefinitely, thus diminishing the secrecy. This creates uncertainty as to whether the information is still a trade secret.

The Law Explanation specifically mentions the Arkansas Trade Secrets Act requiring efforts to maintain secrecy which is undermined by granting a perpetual license. While the provided scraped snippets are not helpful in further understanding Arkansas Trade Secret Law, the Law Explanation is clear and the original and modified texts clearly show the contradiction.
